title: Mitral Regurgitation
oneLiner: Backflow of blood from LV to LA in systole due to valve or LV disease; acute form kills fast, chronic form slowly damages LV.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute severe MR (e.g. papillary muscle rupture post-MI):** sudden pulmonary oedema + new holosystolic murmur + hypotension → treat as cardiogenic shock, call cardiothoracic surgery immediately
- **Infective endocarditis (IE) with MR:** fever + changing murmur + septic emboli → start empiric antibiotics after blood cultures, obtain urgent TOE
sections.Presentation:
- **Acute:** sudden onset dyspnoea at rest, orthopnoea, pink frothy sputum (pulmonary oedema), hypotension, sinus tachycardia
- **Chronic:** progressive exertional dyspnoea, fatigue, palpitations (common with AF), reduced exercise tolerance
- **Exam:**
  - Pansystolic (holosystolic) murmur loudest at apex, radiates to axilla
  - Murmur **increases with handgrip** (increased afterload) — key feature
  - Displaced, hyperdynamic apex beat (LV volume overload)
  - S3 gallop (rapid LV filling)
  - Signs of left heart failure: bibasal crackles, raised JVP if RVF secondary
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Aortic stenosis:** ejection systolic murmur, radiates to carotids, decreased by handgrip, slow-rising pulse → **splitter: handgrip increases MR murmur, decreases AS murmur**
- **Ventricular septal defect (VSD):** holosystolic murmur at lower left sternal border, may be pan and similar; check for thrill at LLSB, VSD often congenital or post-MI → **splitter: VSD murmur is louder at lower sternal border, MR at apex; VSD does not radiate to axilla**
- **Tricuspid regurgitation:** pansystolic at left lower sternal border, increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign), signs of right heart failure → **splitter: Carvallo sign + lack of ↑ with handgrip**
- **Physiological murmur:** usually systolic, short, soft, changes with position, no symptoms → **splitter: normal echo, no cardiomegaly on CXR**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ECG: look for AF, LVH, acute MI (ST changes)
  - Chest X-ray: cardiomegaly (LA + LV enlargement), pulmonary oedema, Kerley B lines (chronic)
  - Pulse oximetry: hypoxia
  - Capillary blood gas (if distress): lactate, oxygenation
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, troponin (if acute), BNP / NT-proBNP
  - Blood cultures x3 (if fever/infection suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) — assess severity (regurgitant jet area, vena contracta, EROA), LV function, LA size, pulmonary pressures
  - **Second-line:** transoesophageal echocardiogram (TOE) — if TTE inadequate, IE suspected, or pre-surgical anatomy needed
- **Special tests:**
  - Cardiac MRI (if echo indeterminate, for LV volumes/function)
  - Right heart catheter (rarely, to quantify shunt or pulmonary hypertension)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Severity grading (ASE/EACVI):**
    - Mild: vena contracta <0.3 cm, EROA <0.2 cm², Regurgitant volume <30 mL/beat
    - Severe: vena contracta ≥0.7 cm, EROA ≥0.4 cm², Regurgitant volume ≥60 mL/beat
  - **Symptoms + severe MR → surgical trigger**
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent TTE** (or TOE if IE suspected) in any patient with new MR + haemodynamic compromise or fever
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Acute severe MR with pulmonary oedema:**
    - Sit patient up, high-flow O₂ (consider CPAP/BiPAP)
    - IV furosemide (e.g. 40–80 mg IV bolus) — [check local policy] for dose
    - IV vasodilator (e.g. GTN infusion or sublingual GTN) if systolic BP >90 mmHg — reduces afterload, decreases regurgitant volume
    - **Do NOT give vasodilators if hypotensive** — consider inotropes (dobutamine) + discuss with senior/cardiothoracic
    - Urgent cardiology and cardiothoracic surgery referral — many need emergency valve surgery
  - **If IE suspected:** start empiric antibiotics after cultures (following local guidelines, e.g. flucloxacillin + gentamicin or vancomycin)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Chronic MR with heart failure:**
    - Titrate diuretics (furosemide oral/IV) for fluid balance
    - Start ACE inhibitor (e.g. ramipril 1.25–2.5 mg BD) to reduce LV afterload — [check local policy] for starting dose and titration
    - Consider beta-blocker (e.g. bisoprolol) if LV systolic dysfunction (dilated LV, EF <40%) — start low, titrate slowly
    - Rate control for AF: beta-blocker or digoxin if EF low
  - Arrange elective TTE or TOE if not done
  - Review for surgical candidacy: severe MR + symptoms or LV dysfunction (EF <60% or LVESD >40 mm)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Medical therapy for chronic MR: as above (ACEi ± BB, diuretics PRN)
  - Anticoagulation if AF (using CHA₂DS₂-VASc score): DOAC (e.g. apixaban) preferred
  - Surgical referral: repair is preferred over replacement; criteria include severe MR + any of: symptoms, new-onset AF, pulmonary hypertension (PAP >50 mmHg at rest), LVEF ≤60%, LVESD ≥40 mm
  - Repeat echo at 6–12 monthly intervals if moderate MR, or sooner if symptoms
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** restrict to 1.5–2 L/day total (oral + IV) if fluid overload; if in cardiogenic shock, cautious IV 0.9% NaCl 250 mL over 1h only after senior review
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if no contraindication, e.g. coagulopathy, bleeding)
  - **Diet:** low-salt diet (no added salt, limit processed foods)
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 hourly initially, then 4-hourly if stable; strict fluid balance chart
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **IV furosemide (acute pulmonary oedema):** 40–80 mg IV bolus over 1–2 min; repeat after 1 hour if poor response — [check local policy]
- **Oral furosemide (chronic fluid overload):** start 40 mg once daily, titrate to symptom relief; max typically 120 mg BD
- **Ramipril (ACEi):** start 1.25–2.5 mg BD; target 5 mg BD — [check local policy]
- **Bisoprolol (beta-blocker):** start 1.25 mg OD, titrate slowly to symptom tolerance (max 10 mg OD)
- **DOAC for AF (apixaban):** 5 mg BD (2.5 mg BD if age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, or Cr ≥133 micromol/L) — [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications:**
  - ACEi: avoid if hyperkalaemia, bilateral renal artery stenosis, pregnancy
  - Beta-blockers: avoid in acute decompensation, asthma, second/third degree AV block
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - ACEi: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min; monitor K+ at 1–2 weeks
  - Furosemide: higher doses needed in renal impairment; risk of ototoxicity
  - DOACs: avoid if CrCl <15–25 mL/min (depending on agent)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Vasodilators (GTN) in acute MR with hypotension:** can cause catastrophic drop in BP → cardiac arrest. Always check systolic BP >90 mmHg before giving.
  - **Over-diuresis in acute MR:** removing preload can worsen hypotension; aim for euvolaemia, not dry.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Pulmonary oedema (flash)
  - Cardiogenic shock
  - Papillary muscle rupture → severe acute MR, high mortality
  - Infective endocarditis (if valve tissue exposed)
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive LV dilatation → systolic dysfunction (heart failure with reduced EF)
  - Atrial fibrillation (due to LA enlargement)
  - Pulmonary hypertension → right heart failure
  - Stroke (due to AF or IE embolisation)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Acute severe MR:** emergency surgery needed; without intervention, mortality >50% at 24 hours; with surgery, ~20–30% in-hospital mortality
- **Chronic severe MR:** 1–2% annual mortality in asymptomatic with normal LV; once symptoms or LV dysfunction appear, 5-year survival ~30% without surgery; valve repair restores near-normal life expectancy
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do a handgrip test** at bedside — if murmur gets louder, favour MR over AS
- **Listen for an S3 gallop** in chronic MR — indicates rapid LV filling and volume overload, correlates with symptomatic disease
- **Check blood cultures in any new murmur + fever** before starting antibiotics — IE is a common killer
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT give beta-blockers in acute severe MR** — they reduce heart rate and contractility, worsening regurgitation; use rate control only after vasodilators/diuresis
- **Do NOT delay echo in acute dyspnoea + new murmur** — cannot distinguish MR from VSD or AS by auscultation alone
- **Do NOT assume chronic MR is benign** — even asymptomatic severe MR with normal LV should be discussed with surgeon (some guidelines recommend surgery if progressive LV dilatation)
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** increasing breathlessness at rest, waking gasping at night, new palpitations or chest pain, or fever/chills (suggests IE)
- **Follow-up:** cardiology outpatient within 2–4 weeks; repeat echo at 6–12 months
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (or med reg) if:** NEWS2 ≥5, any signs of acute pulmonary oedema, hypotension (systolic BP <90 mmHg), lactate >2 mmol/L, new GCS <15
- **Call ICU if:** requiring CPAP/BiPAP or >4 L O₂, inotropic support, or impending cardiogenic shock
- **Call cardiothoracic surgery if:** confirmed acute severe MR with haemodynamic instability or papillary muscle rupture on echo
- **Crash call (2222):** unresponsive, agonal breathing, periarrest rhythm

rawMarkdown:
## Mitral Regurgitation
**One-liner:** Backflow of blood from LV to LA in systole due to valve or LV disease; acute form kills fast, chronic form slowly damages LV.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute severe MR (e.g. papillary muscle rupture post-MI):** sudden pulmonary oedema + new holosystolic murmur + hypotension → treat as cardiogenic shock, call cardiothoracic surgery immediately
- **Infective endocarditis (IE) with MR:** fever + changing murmur + septic emboli → start empiric antibiotics after blood cultures, obtain urgent TOE

### Presentation
- **Acute:** sudden onset dyspnoea at rest, orthopnoea, pink frothy sputum (pulmonary oedema), hypotension, sinus tachycardia
- **Chronic:** progressive exertional dyspnoea, fatigue, palpitations (common with AF), reduced exercise tolerance
- **Exam:**
  - Pansystolic (holosystolic) murmur loudest at apex, radiates to axilla
  - Murmur **increases with handgrip** (increased afterload) — key feature
  - Displaced, hyperdynamic apex beat (LV volume overload)
  - S3 gallop (rapid LV filling)
  - Signs of left heart failure: bibasal crackles, raised JVP if RVF secondary

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Aortic stenosis:** ejection systolic murmur, radiates to carotids, decreased by handgrip, slow-rising pulse → **splitter: handgrip increases MR murmur, decreases AS murmur**
- **Ventricular septal defect (VSD):** holosystolic murmur at lower left sternal border, may be pan and similar; check for thrill at LLSB, VSD often congenital or post-MI → **splitter: VSD murmur is louder at lower sternal border, MR at apex; VSD does not radiate to axilla**
- **Tricuspid regurgitation:** pansystolic at left lower sternal border, increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign), signs of right heart failure → **splitter: Carvallo sign + lack of ↑ with handgrip**
- **Physiological murmur:** usually systolic, short, soft, changes with position, no symptoms → **splitter: normal echo, no cardiomegaly on CXR**

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ECG: look for AF, LVH, acute MI (ST changes)
  - Chest X-ray: cardiomegaly (LA + LV enlargement), pulmonary oedema, Kerley B lines (chronic)
  - Pulse oximetry: hypoxia
  - Capillary blood gas (if distress): lactate, oxygenation
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, troponin (if acute), BNP / NT-proBNP
  - Blood cultures x3 (if fever/infection suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) — assess severity (regurgitant jet area, vena contracta, EROA), LV function, LA size, pulmonary pressures
  - **Second-line:** transoesophageal echocardiogram (TOE) — if TTE inadequate, IE suspected, or pre-surgical anatomy needed
- **Special tests:**
  - Cardiac MRI (if echo indeterminate, for LV volumes/function)
  - Right heart catheter (rarely, to quantify shunt or pulmonary hypertension)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Severity grading (ASE/EACVI):**
    - Mild: vena contracta <0.3 cm, EROA <0.2 cm², Regurgitant volume <30 mL/beat
    - Severe: vena contracta ≥0.7 cm, EROA ≥0.4 cm², Regurgitant volume ≥60 mL/beat
  - **Symptoms + severe MR → surgical trigger**
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent TTE** (or TOE if IE suspected) in any patient with new MR + haemodynamic compromise or fever

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Acute severe MR with pulmonary oedema:**
    - Sit patient up, high-flow O₂ (consider CPAP/BiPAP)
    - IV furosemide (e.g. 40–80 mg IV bolus) — [check local policy] for dose
    - IV vasodilator (e.g. GTN infusion or sublingual GTN) if systolic BP >90 mmHg — reduces afterload, decreases regurgitant volume
    - **Do NOT give vasodilators if hypotensive** — consider inotropes (dobutamine) + discuss with senior/cardiothoracic
    - Urgent cardiology and cardiothoracic surgery referral — many need emergency valve surgery
  - **If IE suspected:** start empiric antibiotics after cultures (following local guidelines, e.g. flucloxacillin + gentamicin or vancomycin)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Chronic MR with heart failure:**
    - Titrate diuretics (furosemide oral/IV) for fluid balance
    - Start ACE inhibitor (e.g. ramipril 1.25–2.5 mg BD) to reduce LV afterload — [check local policy] for starting dose and titration
    - Consider beta-blocker (e.g. bisoprolol) if LV systolic dysfunction (dilated LV, EF <40%) — start low, titrate slowly
    - Rate control for AF: beta-blocker or digoxin if EF low
  - Arrange elective TTE or TOE if not done
  - Review for surgical candidacy: severe MR + symptoms or LV dysfunction (EF <60% or LVESD >40 mm)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Medical therapy for chronic MR: as above (ACEi ± BB, diuretics PRN)
  - Anticoagulation if AF (using CHA₂DS₂-VASc score): DOAC (e.g. apixaban) preferred
  - Surgical referral: repair is preferred over replacement; criteria include severe MR + any of: symptoms, new-onset AF, pulmonary hypertension (PAP >50 mmHg at rest), LVEF ≤60%, LVESD ≥40 mm
  - Repeat echo at 6–12 monthly intervals if moderate MR, or sooner if symptoms
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** restrict to 1.5–2 L/day total (oral + IV) if fluid overload; if in cardiogenic shock, cautious IV 0.9% NaCl 250 mL over 1h only after senior review
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if no contraindication, e.g. coagulopathy, bleeding)
  - **Diet:** low-salt diet (no added salt, limit processed foods)
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 hourly initially, then 4-hourly if stable; strict fluid balance chart

### Drugs & Doses
- **IV furosemide (acute pulmonary oedema):** 40–80 mg IV bolus over 1–2 min; repeat after 1 hour if poor response — [check local policy]
- **Oral furosemide (chronic fluid overload):** start 40 mg once daily, titrate to symptom relief; max typically 120 mg BD
- **Ramipril (ACEi):** start 1.25–2.5 mg BD; target 5 mg BD — [check local policy]
- **Bisoprolol (beta-blocker):** start 1.25 mg OD, titrate slowly to symptom tolerance (max 10 mg OD)
- **DOAC for AF (apixaban):** 5 mg BD (2.5 mg BD if age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, or Cr ≥133 micromol/L) — [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications:**
  - ACEi: avoid if hyperkalaemia, bilateral renal artery stenosis, pregnancy
  - Beta-blockers: avoid in acute decompensation, asthma, second/third degree AV block
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - ACEi: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min; monitor K+ at 1–2 weeks
  - Furosemide: higher doses needed in renal impairment; risk of ototoxicity
  - DOACs: avoid if CrCl <15–25 mL/min (depending on agent)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Vasodilators (GTN) in acute MR with hypotension:** can cause catastrophic drop in BP → cardiac arrest. Always check systolic BP >90 mmHg before giving.
  - **Over-diuresis in acute MR:** removing preload can worsen hypotension; aim for euvolaemia, not dry.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Pulmonary oedema (flash)
  - Cardiogenic shock
  - Papillary muscle rupture → severe acute MR, high mortality
  - Infective endocarditis (if valve tissue exposed)
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive LV dilatation → systolic dysfunction (heart failure with reduced EF)
  - Atrial fibrillation (due to LA enlargement)
  - Pulmonary hypertension → right heart failure
  - Stroke (due to AF or IE embolisation)

### Prognosis
- **Acute severe MR:** emergency surgery needed; without intervention, mortality >50% at 24 hours; with surgery, ~20–30% in-hospital mortality
- **Chronic severe MR:** 1–2% annual mortality in asymptomatic with normal LV; once symptoms or LV dysfunction appear, 5-year survival ~30% without surgery; valve repair restores near-normal life expectancy

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do a handgrip test** at bedside — if murmur gets louder, favour MR over AS
- **Listen for an S3 gallop** in chronic MR — indicates rapid LV filling and volume overload, correlates with symptomatic disease
- **Check blood cultures in any new murmur + fever** before starting antibiotics — IE is a common killer

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT give beta-blockers in acute severe MR** — they reduce heart rate and contractility, worsening regurgitation; use rate control only after vasodilators/diuresis
- **Do NOT delay echo in acute dyspnoea + new murmur** — cannot distinguish MR from VSD or AS by auscultation alone
- **Do NOT assume chronic MR is benign** — even asymptomatic severe MR with normal LV should be discussed with surgeon (some guidelines recommend surgery if progressive LV dilatation)

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** increasing breathlessness at rest, waking gasping at night, new palpitations or chest pain, or fever/chills (suggests IE)
- **Follow-up:** cardiology outpatient within 2–4 weeks; repeat echo at 6–12 months

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (or med reg) if:** NEWS2 ≥5, any signs of acute pulmonary oedema, hypotension (systolic BP <90 mmHg), lactate >2 mmol/L, new GCS <15
- **Call ICU if:** requiring CPAP/BiPAP or >4 L O₂, inotropic support, or impending cardiogenic shock
- **Call cardiothoracic surgery if:** confirmed acute severe MR with haemodynamic instability or papillary muscle rupture on echo
- **Crash call (2222):** unresponsive, agonal breathing, periarrest rhythm
